GLOBAL BOND CRISIS IS BACK.

JAPAN 10-YEAR: 2.82%, highest in almost 30 years

JAPAN 30-YEAR: 3.99%, highest in almost 27 years

UK 10-YEAR: 3.2%, highest in almost 18 years

FRANCE 10-YEAR: 3.98%, highest since 2009

This was triggered by Brent crude jumping from $70 to over $100 this month as Middle East tensions escalate. 

Higher oil means higher inflation, which means central banks stay hawkish or hike again. 

That raises borrowing costs everywhere at once, and pressures stocks, especially expensive tech names.

Normally, war pushes investors into bonds for safety. This time, inflation fear is winning instead, and investors are demanding higher returns to hold government debt at all.

$btc - FOMC reversal

Why we countertrend shorted above 66k - confluence 1 (update and extension)

Nice development so far on our FOMC reversal interpretation, where I said we would top out before, not after FOMC.

How far can it go? FOMC rev gives no exact target, only an entry. The entry is the hardest part though and we successfully completed that now. What we do observe on 4 data points, is that it runs through between a 7 - 15% excursion. Keep in mind, 4 data points is low so this is not a unanimous conclusion, it's just to set expectations.

Nonetheless, our short entry stands strong, and we brought it home well so far.

Before, after, one, and done. Three W's in a row.

#LaurusLabs

The management of a mobility engineering company, on their earnings call, said that the mentality of a few people in India is so low that they want you to fail somehow so that they can jump on their soapbox and claim brownie points with "I told you so." 

The mocking and negativity that Laurus Labs faced during 2023 and 2024 were insane. They kept explaining the rationale behind the capital allocation, but people ignored that and purely focused on the reported low EBITDA, CDMO sales drop, and ROCE compression, among other low/negative attributes in the published numbers.

One of the costliest mistakes in investing is judging a business on things management can't actually control.

Exact revenue guidance. A directional outlook is fine.

Regulatory approvals? Who can predict those?

Customer decisions take their own time, and management cannot influence them through talk.

Clinical timelines. You seed a Phase 2 drug development programme today and wait for at least five years before a ~30% probability of success. Now model that in your DCF!

The timing of commercial launches. It takes a few years to scale up from day one of a commercial launch. Nothing works in a jiffy in deep-tech manufacturing. 99% of social media will never get it; everyone wants instant gratification. Bhaw Bhagwaan Che!

None of those are really in management's hands.

What can they control?

Capability. Manufacturing. Capital allocation. Culture.

Page 231 of Framing Business Uncertainty (pinned on my profile) came from a 2.5-hour discussion with Laurus Labs' Dr Satyanarayana Chava and CFO Ravi Kumar in November 2023.

I wasn't there to hear forecasts.

I wanted to understand how they thought, how they built capability, and how they allocated capital.

I wasn't trying to predict the next quarter.

I was trying to understand what the business could look like ten years from then.

That simple shift in thinking changed the way I invest. The ideas in the book aren't about one company or one industry. They're thinking tools for making better decisions whenever the future is uncertain.

Disclaimer: Invested for the very long term. No view on the stock.

Tokenized stocks are finally getting a real shot at the US market. 

Oasis Pro Markets from Ondo now has FINRA clearance to offer them along with ETFs to American investors. 

The whole setup runs under SEC and FINRA rules, so it covers OTC trades, primary offerings, and more. 

Settlement works with regular money or stablecoins, even wallet-to-wallet, and folks can reach them through existing brokers. 

Wonder how soon we’ll see this stuff show up in everyday portfolios.

5 Months later
India Inc is reporting its best earnings quarter in past 2 years

$BTC slipped under $65K today and honestly the chart is the least interesting part.

Real driver is Iran.

Days of escalation, Trump blaming Tehran for Houthi strikes on Saudi vessels, and risk assets cracked.

S&P down 1.2%, Nasdaq down 2.2%, Brent over $100 for the first time since early June.

Crypto is merely downstream here.

What actually got my attention is the Fed odds.

July hike probability jumped from ~12% a week ago to near 40% now, with bond yields at 18-month highs.

Traders are split too. Some calling the rally dead below $65K, others watching the 21-day MA as support with $73K still in play if BTC clears $68K.

Geopolitics moved the macro, macro moved the Fed odds, Bitcoin just inherited the volatility.

Curious how much of this dip is actually about Bitcoin at all.

BofA on $INTC (Buy, $160 PT):

“Intel’s Q2 beat/raise earnings call reconfirms the two pillars of our Buy thesis: 

1) External manufacturing (foundry) customer discussions have reached a more tangible stage, leading to capex boost (positive semicaps), and 

2) INTC’s core server CPU business is strongly participating in the ongoing agentic cycle, with data center sales surging 59% YoY, INTC’s best growth rate in the past 15 years. 

We reiterate Buy and continue to view INTC’s US-based leading-edge capacity and strong backing by the White House as long-term competitive moats. 

Risk-wise we must watch for any capital market action to fund the capex, though we note INTC has other levers re non-core asset disposals, plus potential customer prepayments as alternatives.”

BREAKING:

Google just disclosed a $94,000,000,000 stake in SpaceX.
4.9% of the company. 

Hidden in Alphabet’s earnings disclosure.

The original investment: $1,000,000,000 in 2015.

When SpaceX was valued at $12,000,000,000.
That $1,000,000,000 is now worth $94,000,000,000.

A 94x return.
In 11 years.
While retail lines up to buy SpaceX at $1,750,000,000,000.

Google got in at $12,000,000,000.
The early money made 94x.
The IPO money is hoping for 2x.

That’s the difference between building alongside a company.
And buying the exit.

One of the most valuable investments on Alphabet’s entire balance sheet.
Was a bet on Elon Musk made a decade ago.

The returns that change history.
Are made when nobody else is paying attention.

Jefferies Raises $AMD PT to $640 from $515 - Buy 

Analyst comments: "AMD’s Advancing AI Day delivered on all fronts, with clear leadership in servers, while AI has become much more real with two additional key customers and a roadmap that potentially puts AMD ahead of $NVDA. 

Helios will begin ramping in September, with AI revenue likely well ahead of our $26 billion estimate next year. Our build points to $80–90 billion in calendar 2028, versus our prior $36 billion estimate, assuming multiple gigawatt deployments across the four lead customers.

Both Anthropic and OpenAI discussed a rapid ramp-up of Helios, and the newly introduced ROCm .AI should make that process even easier. AMD matched NVDA in forecasting a $200 billion-plus server market spread across three tiers: headnode, agentic, and general-purpose servers, with agentic approaching 50% of the total addressable market in the out years.

Venice is ramping later this year and puts AMD even further ahead of INTC and ARM competitors. AMD did not commit to a share target beyond saying it would be greater than 50%, but we see upside to $50 billion by calendar 2028, versus our $36 billion estimate.

Overall, there were no major surprises from the day, but rather a consistent message of product leadership in servers and an argument that AMD is closing the gap in AI."

Analyst: Blayne Curtis

There are accusations from the British elite that the American right is falsely painting a picture of Britain as fallen. Here is the reality on the ground as I see it.

Outside the London-Oxford-Cambridge triangle, the economy is BAD. Town centres have been hollowed out. Earnings are low, often places are kept afloat by a few public sector institutions employing people, and the services they require. University towns do better in this respect. The elite are intellectually aware of the cost of living crisis but don’t really get what it’s like. 

The area I live in has changed in the decade I’ve lived here; many shops have closed and the town centre has acquired groups of foreign men milling around. People I talk to say this is common in many towns. In the meantime, where I work in Cambridge has a really nice new train station, and is modern clean and prosperous. 

The middle class wages me and my wife receive felt like they went a lot further in 2020 now. We are doing OK, but we think about money a lot more than we used to. Others we know aren’t doing so well. 

I don’t personally live in one of the areas that’s been hit with significant disorder or a mass influx of migrants - for us it’s more a sense of gradual degradation than immediate crisis. Every year the economy gets worse and there is more crime. And it’s all invisible to the rich parts of the country.
